text: The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to measure and align the terminal ileum and transverse colon for anastomosis. The terminal ileum is cut two centimeters back from the staple line at the anti-mesenteric border using cautery, minimizing the risk of mesenteric damage. On the transverse colon, a colotomy is made six centimeters back from the staple line, slightly off the tinea libra due to epiploic fat presence.

The surgeon inserts the left-hand instrument with one jaw inside the colotomy and one jaw outside, facilitating efficient exposure. The stapler is positioned with its cartridge end inside the colon, aligning the terminal ileum to the stapler. The surgeon drops the butt of the stapler and raises its tip, using a blunt grasper to guide the bowel onto the stapler's crotch. After clamping down, the mesentery is checked for proper alignment and absence of twisting. The stapler is fired, and the surgeon begins sewing the common enterotomy closed, using two Alice clamps to secure the corners of the staple line.

The surgeon performs a full thickness U-stitch on either side of the staple line, taking small bites from the colonic side, first out to in and then in to out. The bottom side suture is emphasized for preventing leaks, with careful exposure of the back wall. The common enterotomy is closed using a 3-0 V-lock suture on a V-20 needle, sewing in two layers from top down and bottom up.

The surgeon begins suturing at the top of the incision using a V-lock suture, with the assistant pulling up on the upper suture to widen the incision for clarity. The surgeon measures the distance between suture exits on the small bowel using a fenestrated bipolar, ensuring each stitch is approximately five millimeters apart to prevent tissue ischemia.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in arm number three to apply inferior traction on the suture, then transfers it to the left hand to complete the closure of the colonic side of the enterotomy. The needle is positioned to poke through the colonic tissue, and the left hand rotates to the left to finish the closure. The surgeon runs a second layer using a running technique, cinching down the V-lock suture after each throw with a needle driver until the barbed tines are no longer visible.

The surgeon uses a finisher to bipolar device to carefully grasp and manipulate the suture, avoiding pinching with the needle driver to prevent fracturing. The suturing continues until reaching the crotch of the anastomosis, where the suture is cut flush with the serosa. Indocyanine green (ICG) is re-injected to visually confirm perfusion of the anastomosis, ensuring no damage has occurred.
